target: WIN-010 and WIN-068: text cleanup after verdict revert from Self-Contradicted back to Std Model Explains

defense_analysis.defense_summary: Eclipse shadow position is an APPARENT phenomenon. The ionospheric Sq current perturbation depends on UV flux changes that track the apparent shadow geometry — where an observer sees the eclipse — not the physical sun altitude. Chapman's mechanism operates in the ionosphere, responding to reduced UV ionization in the shadow path. The n(r) refraction formula affects where an observer perceives the sun, but the UV shadow itself tracks apparent geometry consistently. Therefore the 83% seasonal indeterminacy in physical sun altitude does not propagate to eclipse magnetic predictions.
defense_analysis.why_defense_succeeds: This is correct physics. On the globe, eclipse shadow predictions use apparent solar/lunar geometry (Besselian elements) and these work precisely. The magnetic perturbation tracks the optical shadow, not any physical altitude in dome coordinates. The dome's eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses coverage percentages from standard Besselian elements — which are computed from apparent positions. The n(r) indeterminacy in physical altitude is irrelevant because the formula never requires physical altitude as an input. The human's verdict revert (2026-04-13) was scientifically correct.
remaining_valid_arguments: Chapman ionospheric mechanism (1933) predicts the entire signal independently
No dome equation derives the magnetic perturbation from dome parameters
Post-hoc: 2017 observation adopted retroactively as WIN
FSF values (0.642-2.075, 3.2× spread) claimed to derive from V12 H(r)/r but H(r) varies smoothly with disc radius — stations at similar latitudes should have similar FSF, yet they vary 3.2×
Circular calibration: BOU 2017 -10.9 nT is both a claimed WIN and the baseline input for 2026 predictions
Kp asymmetry: accepts disturbed-day (Kp=3-4) baseline as WIN, requires quiet-day (Kp<2) for future tests

kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies that eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations are real, reproducible, and track eclipse geometry rather than local solar time. The BOU 2017 observation is genuine. The curmudgeon's advocate defense was also correct: the eclipse shadow is an apparent phenomenon, so the n(r) refraction cascade argument does not apply to eclipse-geometry-based predictions. We were wrong to apply Self-Contradicted here.

win_010_patches.finding: Non-discriminating: Chapman ionospheric mechanism (1933) predicts exact signal magnitude and geometry. Dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation; its FSF formula uses the 2017 observation as its own baseline, making it circularly calibrated.
win_010_patches.tldr_evidence: Eclipse-induced magnetic dips have been understood since Chapman (1933) — the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering conductivity and altering Sq currents. The dome's observed -10.9 nT at Boulder is exactly what this standard mechanism predicts.
win_010_patches.tldr_verdict: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism, known for 90+ years, predicts this signal without dome parameters. The dome adds no equation, no derivation — just observes a known phenomenon and claims credit. The 2017 baseline also serves as the input for 2026 predictions, making the calibration circular.
win_010_patches.detail_verdict_text: Non-discriminating: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism — first described by Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) and quantitatively modeled by Meza et al. (2021, JGR Space Physics) — predicts the observed signal magnitude and geometry to within measurement uncertainty without any dome parameters. The mechanism is straightforward: the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-region conductivity and suppressing the Sq (solar quiet) current system. The resulting magnetic perturbation tracks the eclipse shadow path with predictable amplitude and timing. The dome has no equation linking eclipses to magnetic perturbations; it simply observes that the signal exists and claims it. The dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses the BOU 2017 -10.9 nT value as both a claimed WIN and the baseline input for future predictions — the same number counted twice: once as evidence and once as a parameter. In science, training data cannot serve as test data. The FSF (field strength factor) values range from 0.642 to 2.075 across nine stations — a 3.2× spread within 20° of European latitude. The dome claims FSF derives from "V12 H(r)/r dome geometry," but H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) varies smoothly with disc radius and cannot produce a 3.2× spread among stations at similar disc radii. The FSF values are empirically fitted to observed eclipse data, not derived from dome parameters. The Kp precondition creates an asymmetric evidentiary standard: the 2017 baseline underlying this WIN was recorded at Kp = 3–4 (geomagnetically disturbed), yet the dome's monitor.py imposes Kp < 2 for 2026 eclipse validation. The model accepts a disturbed-day observation as a confirmed WIN but requires quiet-day conditions for its own future tests. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from the public INTERMAGNET BOU archive, available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history.

win_068_patches.finding: Non-discriminating: Chapman (1933) ionospheric mechanism predicts eclipse magnetic perturbations across all 39 eclipses in the ensemble. Dome adds no equation and triple-counts the same phenomenon as WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068.
win_068_patches.detail_verdict_text_note: WIN-068 detail_verdict_text also needs rewrite: remove Self-Contradicted framing (refraction cascade), replace with Std Model Explains argument. Lead with: ensemble of 39 eclipses all consistent with Chapman mechanism. No dome equation for eclipse-magnetic coupling. Triple-counting WIN-010/025/068 inflates count. FSF fitting is empirical, not derived from dome geometry.

holes_addressed: Critical: verdict-text mismatch (verdict=Std Model Explains but all text says Self-Contradicted)
Critical: duplicated paragraph in WIN-010 detail_evidence removed by replacement
Major: TLDR inconsistency resolved (TLDRs now match Std Model Explains verdict)
Major: defense neutralization completed — defense accepted, text updated accordingly
Minor: finding field updated to match current verdict

anticipated_objections.objection: If the defense won, why not just remove WIN-010 from the review entirely?
anticipated_objections.response: The defense only invalidated the Self-Contradicted verdict. The Std Model Explains argument remains strong: Chapman mechanism predicts the signal, dome has no equation, FSF is empirical, and the claim is post-hoc. We are not claiming the eclipse observation is wrong — we are claiming the dome adds nothing to explain it.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome claims FSF derives from V12 H(r)/r geometry — shouldnt we investigate this further?
anticipated_objections.response: We note the claim and provide the evidence against it: FSF varies 3.2× across stations at similar disc radii, but H(r) depends only on disc radius. If the dome published the FSF derivation, we could evaluate it. Until then, the 3.2× spread is evidence of empirical fitting.

anticipated_objections.objection: Accepting the defense undermines our refraction cascade argument (EXP-078) across other WINs.
anticipated_objections.response: No. The cascade applies where the dome explicitly needs physical sun altitude for its predictions (analemma, solar elevation angles, crepuscular ray calculations). Eclipse magnetic effects track apparent shadow geometry, which is a genuinely different physical mechanism. The cascade is valid where it applies; it just does not apply to eclipse magnetic predictions.
